title: Statistical Learning
text:
Preface
These are notes for STAT 3113 Regression 2026 Summer at Arkansas Tech University. If you have any questions please contact me through email.

title: Statistical Learning
text:
References


[1] Wickham, H.
(2014). Tidy data.
Journal of Statistical Software 59.


[2] Mendenhall, W.
and Sincich, T. (2020). A second
course in statistics: Regression analyisis. Pearson Education, Inc,
[Hoboken, New Jersey].


[3] Healy, K.
(2025). Gssrdoc: General
social survey documentation for use in r.

title: 1  Basics
text:
1.1 Quick overview
Given a set of observations of variables, we would like to understand the relationships among these variables.
The standard procedure for developing a statistical model is as follows:
There are two primary goals and related ways to assess the model:
Both Statistical Learning and specific methods like Regression Analysis may use both assessment strategies, but the emphasis differs. In this course, the focus is on the principles of modeling and statistical inference, with prediction discussed but not treated as the primary objective.

title: 1  Basics
text:
Proposing a model informed by exploratory data analysis and relevant domain knowledge.
Estimating the parameters of the proposed model using the available data.
Assessing the adequacy and quality of the fitted model.
Revising the model based on the assessment results.



Prediction (Generalization): We evaluate the fitted model on unseen data, using metrics like MSE or classification error. This assesses the model’s generalization ability, which is the primary focus of subjects like Statistical Learning or Machine Learning.
Explanation (Inference): We use measures such as \(R^2\) to assess the goodness-of-fit of the fitted model, and we use confidence intervals and p-values to perform statistical inference about population parameters. Although these calculations are based on the observed sample, they are interpreted as statements about the population. This approach focuses on understanding relationships among variables and is central to traditional Statistical Modeling, including Regression Analysis.

title: 1  Basics
text:
1.2 Population vs Sample

Population: the set of all elements of interest in a particular study.
Sample: is a subset of the population
Statistical Inference: The process of using data collected on a sample to draw conclusions about a population is called statistical inference.

A population is described by parameters (e.g., \(\mu\), \(\sigma\), \(\beta\)). A sample produces statistics (e.g., \(\bar X\), \(s\), \(\hat\beta\)), which vary across repeated samples. Statistical inference uses the sampling distribution of these statistics to draw conclusions about population parameters. This part will be revisited later in ?sec-inference.

Example 1.1 (gssr)  


Click to expand.

According to a previous national media report, the average age of U.S. adults who watch national TV news is 60 years. A data analyst hypothesizes that the average age of frequent TV-news watchers is greater than 60. To test this, she uses the General Social Survey (GSS), accessible via the gssr R package [1], to draw a sample of 500 respondents and record their ages.

Describe the population: All U.S. adults who watch TV news several times a week or more often, as defined by the GSS survey question on TV-news frequency.
Describe the sample: A random sample of 500 GSS respondents who meet the above criterion and have valid age data.
Sample size: \(n = 500\)
Describe the statistical inference: We want to determine whether the mean age of frequent TV-news watchers is greater than 60 based on the sampled data.

title: 1  Basics
text:
1.3 Data structure
A dataset is a collection of values. Values are organized in two ways. Every value belongs to a variable and an observation.

Definition 1.1 (Variables and Observations [2])  

A variable contains all values that measure the same underlying attribute (like height, temperature, duration) across units.
An observation contains all values measured on the same unit (like a person, or a day, or a race) across attributes.



Example 1.2 (possum)  


Click to expand.

Researchers collected body measurements for bushtail possums in Eastern Australia. They trapped 104 possums and recorded location where possum was trapped, age, gender, head length, skull width, total length, tail length, etc. for each possum. For more details see ?sec-app_possum.

library(DAAG)
data(possum)
head(possum)
##     case site Pop sex age hdlngth skullw totlngth taill footlgth earconch  eye
## C3     1    1 Vic   m   8    94.1   60.4     89.0  36.0     74.5     54.5 15.2
## C5     2    1 Vic   f   6    92.5   57.6     91.5  36.5     72.5     51.2 16.0
## C10    3    1 Vic   f   6    94.0   60.0     95.5  39.0     75.4     51.9 15.5
## C15    4    1 Vic   f   6    93.2   57.1     92.0  38.0     76.1     52.2 15.2
## C23    5    1 Vic   f   2    91.5   56.3     85.5  36.0     71.0     53.2 15.1
## C24    6    1 Vic   f   1    93.1   54.8     90.5  35.5     73.2     53.6 14.2
##     chest belly
## C3   28.0    36
## C5   28.5    33
## C10  30.0    34
## C15  28.0    34
## C23  28.5    33
## C24  30.0    32

The dimension of the dataset is

dim(possum)
## [1] 104  14

In this example, there are 14 variables, and 104 observations. Each column is a variable. In R you can use the column name to get access to the variable.

possum$hdlngth
##   [1]  94.1  92.5  94.0  93.2  91.5  93.1  95.3  94.8  93.4  91.8  93.3  94.9
##  [13]  95.1  95.4  92.9  91.6  94.7  93.5  94.4  94.8  95.9  96.3  92.5  94.4
##  [25]  95.8  96.0  90.5  93.8  92.8  92.1  92.8  94.3  91.4  90.6  94.4  93.3
##  [37]  89.3  92.4  84.7  91.0  88.4  85.3  90.0  85.1  90.7  91.4  90.1  98.6
##  [49]  95.4  91.6  95.6  97.6  93.1  96.9 103.1  99.9  95.1  94.5 102.5  91.3
##  [61]  95.7  91.3  92.0  96.9  93.5  90.4  93.3  94.1  98.0  91.9  92.8  85.9
##  [73]  82.5  88.7  93.8  92.4  93.6  86.5  85.8  86.7  90.6  86.0  90.0  88.4
##  [85]  89.5  88.2  98.5  89.6  97.7  92.6  97.8  90.7  89.2  91.8  91.6  94.8
##  [97]  91.0  93.2  93.3  89.5  88.6  92.4  91.5  93.6




Definition 1.2 (Quantitative and Qualitative [3])  

Quantitative data are observations measured on a naturally occurring numerical scale. It is also called numerical data.
Qualitative data are nonnumerical data that can only be classified into one of a group of categories. It is also called categorical data.


In regression, qualitative variables must be encoded using indicator (dummy) variables.

Definition 1.3 (Discrete and Continuous [3])  

Discrete random variable: A random variable that assumes either a finite number of values or an infinite sequence of values such as 0, 1, 2…
Continuous random variable: A random variable that may assume any numerical value in an interval or collection of intervals.







graph TD
    A[All Variables] --> B[Numerical]
    A --> C[Categorical]
    B --> D[Continuous]
    B --> E[Discrete]












NoteRandom variables



A variable in a dataset can be modeled by a random variable. The probability density function / probability mass function of the random variable can describe the distribution of all possible values of the variable in a dataset.
Making a measurement is equivalent to taking a sample from the corresponding random variable. This will be discussed in detail in ?sec-inference.

title: 1  Basics
text:
1.4 Data Visualization

1.4.1 Qualitative (categorical) data
Usually the most important is the class relative frequency: \[
\text{class relative frequency}=\frac{\text{class frequency}}{n}.
\]
To display it, we could use table, bar chart or pie chart.

Example 1.3 (possum)  


Click to expand.

Here we still consider the possum dataset.

library(DAAG)
data(possum)
head(possum)
##     case site Pop sex age hdlngth skullw totlngth taill footlgth earconch  eye
## C3     1    1 Vic   m   8    94.1   60.4     89.0  36.0     74.5     54.5 15.2
## C5     2    1 Vic   f   6    92.5   57.6     91.5  36.5     72.5     51.2 16.0
## C10    3    1 Vic   f   6    94.0   60.0     95.5  39.0     75.4     51.9 15.5
## C15    4    1 Vic   f   6    93.2   57.1     92.0  38.0     76.1     52.2 15.2
## C23    5    1 Vic   f   2    91.5   56.3     85.5  36.0     71.0     53.2 15.1
## C24    6    1 Vic   f   1    93.1   54.8     90.5  35.5     73.2     53.6 14.2
##     chest belly
## C3   28.0    36
## C5   28.5    33
## C10  30.0    34
## C15  28.0    34
## C23  28.5    33
## C24  30.0    32

The variable Pop (location where possum was trapped) is qualitative. We could use the following ways to display it. We first compute the frequency table of the variable Pop:

table(possum$Pop)
## 
##   Vic other 
##    46    58

or relative frequency table:

table(possum$Pop)/length(possum$Pop)
## 
##       Vic     other 
## 0.4423077 0.5576923

Then we could draw the barplot of this variable:

barplot(table(possum$Pop))








and the pie plot:

pie(table(possum$Pop))








Note that table is handling the statistics, while barplot and pie draw on top of the result from table.




1.4.2 Quantitative (numerical) data
For quantitative data, we would like to see the histogram, as well as computing some statistics: min, max, quartiles, median and mean.

Histogram is an approximation of the distribution. In other words, we split the range into small segments (called bins), and count the frequency or relative frequency of data falling into these bins.
Box plot consists of a box, two lines and possibly some points:

The box in the box plot extends from the lower quartile to the upper quartile. The difference between the upper quartile and the lower quartile is called the inter-quartile range (IQR).
The lines, known as whiskers, extend to one and a half times the interquartile range, but they stop at the most extreme data points that fall within this range.
The points, considered as outliers, are those which are not covered by the box and the lines.


Histograms and boxplots help check normality and outliers before fitting regression. These summaries approximate the underlying distribution of the variable. In regression, we later apply similar tools to the residuals to check model assumptions.

Example 1.4 (possum)  


Click to expand.

We still consider the possum dataset. We could use summary to compute the major statistics.

library(DAAG)
data(possum)
summary(possum)
##       case             site          Pop     sex         age       
##  Min.   :  1.00   Min.   :1.000   Vic  :46   f:43   Min.   :1.000  
##  1st Qu.: 26.75   1st Qu.:1.000   other:58   m:61   1st Qu.:2.250  
##  Median : 52.50   Median :3.000                     Median :3.000  
##  Mean   : 52.50   Mean   :3.625                     Mean   :3.833  
##  3rd Qu.: 78.25   3rd Qu.:6.000                     3rd Qu.:5.000  
##  Max.   :104.00   Max.   :7.000                     Max.   :9.000  
##                                                     NA's   :2      
##     hdlngth           skullw         totlngth         taill      
##  Min.   : 82.50   Min.   :50.00   Min.   :75.00   Min.   :32.00  
##  1st Qu.: 90.67   1st Qu.:54.98   1st Qu.:84.00   1st Qu.:35.88  
##  Median : 92.80   Median :56.35   Median :88.00   Median :37.00  
##  Mean   : 92.60   Mean   :56.88   Mean   :87.09   Mean   :37.01  
##  3rd Qu.: 94.72   3rd Qu.:58.10   3rd Qu.:90.00   3rd Qu.:38.00  
##  Max.   :103.10   Max.   :68.60   Max.   :96.50   Max.   :43.00  
##                                                                  
##     footlgth        earconch          eye            chest          belly      
##  Min.   :60.30   Min.   :40.30   Min.   :12.80   Min.   :22.0   Min.   :25.00  
##  1st Qu.:64.60   1st Qu.:44.80   1st Qu.:14.40   1st Qu.:25.5   1st Qu.:31.00  
##  Median :68.00   Median :46.80   Median :14.90   Median :27.0   Median :32.50  
##  Mean   :68.46   Mean   :48.13   Mean   :15.05   Mean   :27.0   Mean   :32.59  
##  3rd Qu.:72.50   3rd Qu.:52.00   3rd Qu.:15.72   3rd Qu.:28.0   3rd Qu.:34.12  
##  Max.   :77.90   Max.   :56.20   Max.   :17.80   Max.   :32.0   Max.   :40.00  
##  NA's   :1

The variable hdlngth (head length) is quantitative. We first show the histogram. We could use breaks to control the number of bins. Note that the function hist does not only draw the histogram; it also provides many useful pieces of information.

res <- hist(possum$hdlngth, breaks=10)







res
## $breaks
##  [1]  82  84  86  88  90  92  94  96  98 100 102 104
## 
## $counts
##  [1]  1  6  2 12 22 27 22  7  3  0  2
## 
## $density
##  [1] 0.004807692 0.028846154 0.009615385 0.057692308 0.105769231 0.129807692
##  [7] 0.105769231 0.033653846 0.014423077 0.000000000 0.009615385
## 
## $mids
##  [1]  83  85  87  89  91  93  95  97  99 101 103
## 
## $xname
## [1] "possum$hdlngth"
## 
## $equidist
## [1] TRUE
## 
## attr(,"class")
## [1] "histogram"

Then we could show the box plot. You may compare the lines in the plot with the summary table and the histogram above.

boxplot(possum$hdlngth)












1.4.3 Relations among multiple variables
We could show the relation between two variables in a scatterplot. Scatterplots help us assess linearity, detect outliers, and decide whether transformation is needed.

Example 1.5 (possum)  


Click to expand.

When both variables are numerical and continuous:

plot(possum$hdlngth, possum$skullw)








When one variable is categorical:

plot(as.factor(possum$Pop), possum$skullw)








Note that in this case, the categorical data has to be a factor. And once it is cast into a factor, the plot is multiple box plots for each category. This type of grouped boxplot corresponds to comparing group means and is directly related to one-way ANOVA and regression with dummy variables. It will be discussed in later sections.
We can see pair plots for each pair of variables. Pair plots are very important because they help reveal relationships among predictors. Note that before creating the plot, we have to cast pop and sex into factors.

possum$Pop <- as.factor(possum$Pop)
possum$sex <- as.factor(possum$sex)
pairs(possum)














[1] Healy, K. (2025). Gssrdoc: General social survey documentation for use in r.


[2] Wickham, H. (2014). Tidy data. Journal of Statistical Software 59.


[3] Mendenhall, W. and Sincich, T. (2020). A second course in statistics: Regression analyisis. Pearson Education, Inc, [Hoboken, New Jersey].

title: Appendix A — Quick intro to R
text: A.1 For those who are from other languages, especially Python

title: Appendix A — Quick intro to R
text:
The character . is NOT special. It doesn’t mean packages or classes. It is just one of the characters that can be used as variable names.
R recommends to use <- to denote assigning values, although = is accepted. a <- 1 and a = 1 are essentially the same. It has some benefits

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
2 Course Narrative
This course is organized as an introduction to statistical learning from a statistical perspective.
The focus is on three recurring ideas:
The course moves from simple predictive models to more flexible methods, then to evaluation tools, regularization, ensemble methods, and practical workflow.
The course follows this progression:
This gives students a coherent view of statistical learning as a modeling pipeline rather than a disconnected list of techniques.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
4.1 Main Topics

What is statistical learning?
Supervised vs unsupervised learning
Parametric vs nonparametric methods
Prediction vs explanation
Bias–variance tradeoff

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
4.2 Teaching Goals
Students should understand that the main goal of statistical learning is good prediction on new data, not simply fitting the observed sample as closely as possible.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
4.3 Suggested Emphasis

Introduce the overall modeling problem first.
Use simple real-world examples such as house prices, medical diagnosis, or spam filtering.
Make overfitting a central theme from the beginning.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
5.1 Main Topics

Linear regression as a prediction tool
Interpreting fitted relationships
Strengths and weaknesses of linear models
Linear models as a baseline for comparison

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
5.2 Teaching Goals
Students should see linear regression as the starting point of predictive modeling: simple, interpretable, and useful, but limited in flexibility.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
5.3 Suggested Emphasis

Present linear regression mainly as a predictive model.
Keep inference light unless needed for context.
Highlight when linear models fail to capture nonlinear structure.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
6.1 Main Topics

Flexible regression methods
K-nearest neighbors regression
Smoothers and local fitting ideas
Model flexibility and overfitting

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
6.2 Teaching Goals
Students should learn that more flexible models can better capture complicated patterns, but at the cost of increased variance.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
6.3 Suggested Emphasis

Connect this unit directly to bias–variance tradeoff.
Compare flexible and inflexible models visually.
Use plots to show underfitting and overfitting.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
7.1 Main Topics

Classification as a prediction problem
Logistic regression
Decision boundaries
K-nearest neighbors classification

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
7.2 Teaching Goals
Students should understand that classification uses the same modeling logic as regression, but with categorical outcomes.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
7.3 Suggested Emphasis

Stress the difference between predicting a class and predicting a numeric response.
Introduce class probabilities early.
Use confusion matrices and classification error as evaluation tools.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
8.1 Main Topics

Logistic regression in more detail
Interpreting probabilities
Thresholds and classification decisions
Comparing classifiers

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
8.2 Teaching Goals
Students should learn how binary classification models convert predictors into class probabilities and final decisions.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
8.3 Suggested Emphasis

Distinguish between probability estimation and hard classification.
Discuss how different thresholds change predictions.
Use practical examples such as disease detection or spam filtering.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
9.1 Main Topics

LDA
QDA
Naive Bayes
Discriminative vs generative modeling

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
9.2 Teaching Goals
Students should understand the idea of modeling class distributions directly and then using Bayes’ rule for prediction.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
9.3 Suggested Emphasis

Compare logistic regression with LDA and QDA conceptually.
Emphasize assumptions and when they matter.
Use side-by-side model comparisons.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
10.1 Main Topics

Validation set approach
Cross-validation
Bootstrap
Estimating test error

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
10.2 Teaching Goals
This unit is the conceptual center of the course. Students must understand that training error is not enough, and that model performance must be evaluated using principled estimates of test error.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
10.3 Suggested Emphasis

Make cross-validation a central practical tool.
Show why naive comparison by training error is misleading.
Use repeated examples across multiple methods.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
11.1 Main Topics

Ridge regression
Lasso
Elastic net
Shrinkage and complexity control

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
11.2 Teaching Goals
Students should understand how regularization controls complexity and improves prediction without exhaustively searching across models.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
11.3 Suggested Emphasis

Present regularization as a modern answer to overfitting in linear models.
Compare subset selection and shrinkage conceptually.
Use coefficient path plots when possible.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
12.1 Main Topics

Decision trees
Bagging
Random forests
Boosting

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
12.2 Teaching Goals
Students should learn that combining many unstable or weak learners can create a stronger predictive model.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
12.3 Suggested Emphasis

Start with a single tree and its limitations.
Then motivate bagging as variance reduction.
Finally introduce random forests and boosting as improved ensemble ideas.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
13.1 Main Topics

End-to-end modeling workflow
Data preprocessing
Model tuning
Model comparison
Practical considerations on real datasets

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
13.2 Teaching Goals
Students should be able to complete a full predictive analysis from data setup through final model comparison and interpretation.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
13.3 Suggested Emphasis

Organize this unit around a full case study.
Show how preprocessing, resampling, tuning, and comparison fit together.
Emphasize reproducible workflow and communication.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
14.1 Midterm I
Suggested coverage:

foundations of statistical learning,
linear regression,
nonparametric regression,
early classification concepts.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
14.2 Midterm II
Suggested coverage:

classification,
generative models,
resampling,
regularization.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
14.3 Final Assessment or Project
Suggested format:

a real dataset,
several competing methods,
model comparison using validation or cross-validation,
written interpretation of results.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
15.1 Lecture 1

concept and intuition,
visual explanation,
strengths and limitations of the method.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
15.2 Lecture 2

implementation,
model fitting,
comparison with earlier methods,
short applied example.

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
16.1 Theme 1: Model Flexibility
Students should repeatedly ask:

How flexible is this model, and what does that flexibility buy us?

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
16.2 Theme 2: Generalization
Students should repeatedly ask:

How do we know whether this model will work on new data?

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
16.3 Theme 3: Comparison
Students should repeatedly ask:

Is this method better than what we already had?

title: 2  Linear regression
text:
2.1 Quick overview
Given a set of observations of variables, we would like to understand the relationships among these variables.
The standard procedure for developing a statistical model is as follows:
There are two primary goals and related ways to assess the model:
Both Statistical Learning and specific methods like Regression Analysis may use both assessment strategies, but the emphasis differs. In this course, the focus is on the principles of modeling and statistical inference, with prediction discussed but not treated as the primary objective.

title: 2  Linear regression
text:
Proposing a model informed by exploratory data analysis and relevant domain knowledge.
Estimating the parameters of the proposed model using the available data.
Assessing the adequacy and quality of the fitted model.
Revising the model based on the assessment results.



Prediction (Generalization): We evaluate the fitted model on unseen data, using metrics like MSE or classification error. This assesses the model’s generalization ability, which is the primary focus of subjects like Statistical Learning or Machine Learning.
Explanation (Inference): We use measures such as \(R^2\) to assess the goodness-of-fit of the fitted model, and we use confidence intervals and p-values to perform statistical inference about population parameters. Although these calculations are based on the observed sample, they are interpreted as statements about the population. This approach focuses on understanding relationships among variables and is central to traditional Statistical Modeling, including Regression Analysis.

title: 2  Linear regression
text:
2.2 Population vs Sample

Population: the set of all elements of interest in a particular study.
Sample: is a subset of the population
Statistical Inference: The process of using data collected on a sample to draw conclusions about a population is called statistical inference.

A population is described by parameters (e.g., \(\mu\), \(\sigma\), \(\beta\)). A sample produces statistics (e.g., \(\bar X\), \(s\), \(\hat\beta\)), which vary across repeated samples. Statistical inference uses the sampling distribution of these statistics to draw conclusions about population parameters. This part will be revisited later in ?sec-inference.

Example 2.1 (gssr)  


Click to expand.

According to a previous national media report, the average age of U.S. adults who watch national TV news is 60 years. A data analyst hypothesizes that the average age of frequent TV-news watchers is greater than 60. To test this, she uses the General Social Survey (GSS), accessible via the gssr R package [1], to draw a sample of 500 respondents and record their ages.

Describe the population: All U.S. adults who watch TV news several times a week or more often, as defined by the GSS survey question on TV-news frequency.
Describe the sample: A random sample of 500 GSS respondents who meet the above criterion and have valid age data.
Sample size: \(n = 500\)
Describe the statistical inference: We want to determine whether the mean age of frequent TV-news watchers is greater than 60 based on the sampled data.

title: 2  Linear regression
text:
2.3 Data structure
A dataset is a collection of values. Values are organized in two ways. Every value belongs to a variable and an observation.

Definition 2.1 (Variables and Observations [2])  

A variable contains all values that measure the same underlying attribute (like height, temperature, duration) across units.
An observation contains all values measured on the same unit (like a person, or a day, or a race) across attributes.



Example 2.2 (possum)  


Click to expand.

Researchers collected body measurements for bushtail possums in Eastern Australia. They trapped 104 possums and recorded location where possum was trapped, age, gender, head length, skull width, total length, tail length, etc. for each possum. For more details see ?sec-app_possum.

library(DAAG)
data(possum)
head(possum)

    case site Pop sex age hdlngth skullw totlngth taill footlgth earconch  eye
C3     1    1 Vic   m   8    94.1   60.4     89.0  36.0     74.5     54.5 15.2
C5     2    1 Vic   f   6    92.5   57.6     91.5  36.5     72.5     51.2 16.0
C10    3    1 Vic   f   6    94.0   60.0     95.5  39.0     75.4     51.9 15.5
C15    4    1 Vic   f   6    93.2   57.1     92.0  38.0     76.1     52.2 15.2
C23    5    1 Vic   f   2    91.5   56.3     85.5  36.0     71.0     53.2 15.1
C24    6    1 Vic   f   1    93.1   54.8     90.5  35.5     73.2     53.6 14.2
    chest belly
C3   28.0    36
C5   28.5    33
C10  30.0    34
C15  28.0    34
C23  28.5    33
C24  30.0    32


The dimension of the dataset is

dim(possum)

[1] 104  14


In this example, there are 14 variables, and 104 observations. Each column is a variable. In R you can use the column name to get access to the variable.

possum$hdlngth

  [1]  94.1  92.5  94.0  93.2  91.5  93.1  95.3  94.8  93.4  91.8  93.3  94.9
 [13]  95.1  95.4  92.9  91.6  94.7  93.5  94.4  94.8  95.9  96.3  92.5  94.4
 [25]  95.8  96.0  90.5  93.8  92.8  92.1  92.8  94.3  91.4  90.6  94.4  93.3
 [37]  89.3  92.4  84.7  91.0  88.4  85.3  90.0  85.1  90.7  91.4  90.1  98.6
 [49]  95.4  91.6  95.6  97.6  93.1  96.9 103.1  99.9  95.1  94.5 102.5  91.3
 [61]  95.7  91.3  92.0  96.9  93.5  90.4  93.3  94.1  98.0  91.9  92.8  85.9
 [73]  82.5  88.7  93.8  92.4  93.6  86.5  85.8  86.7  90.6  86.0  90.0  88.4
 [85]  89.5  88.2  98.5  89.6  97.7  92.6  97.8  90.7  89.2  91.8  91.6  94.8
 [97]  91.0  93.2  93.3  89.5  88.6  92.4  91.5  93.6





Definition 2.2 (Quantitative and Qualitative [3])  

Quantitative data are observations measured on a naturally occurring numerical scale. It is also called numerical data.
Qualitative data are nonnumerical data that can only be classified into one of a group of categories. It is also called categorical data.


In regression, qualitative variables must be encoded using indicator (dummy) variables.

Definition 2.3 (Discrete and Continuous [3])  

Discrete random variable: A random variable that assumes either a finite number of values or an infinite sequence of values such as 0, 1, 2…
Continuous random variable: A random variable that may assume any numerical value in an interval or collection of intervals.







graph TD
    A[All Variables] --> B[Numerical]
    A --> C[Categorical]
    B --> D[Continuous]
    B --> E[Discrete]












NoteRandom variables



A variable in a dataset can be modeled by a random variable. The probability density function / probability mass function of the random variable can describe the distribution of all possible values of the variable in a dataset.
Making a measurement is equivalent to taking a sample from the corresponding random variable. This will be discussed in detail in ?sec-inference.

title: 2  Linear regression
text:
2.4 Data Visualization

2.4.1 Qualitative (categorical) data
Usually the most important is the class relative frequency: \[
\text{class relative frequency}=\frac{\text{class frequency}}{n}.
\]
To display it, we could use table, bar chart or pie chart.

Example 2.3 (possum)  


Click to expand.

Here we still consider the possum dataset.

library(DAAG)
data(possum)
head(possum)

    case site Pop sex age hdlngth skullw totlngth taill footlgth earconch  eye
C3     1    1 Vic   m   8    94.1   60.4     89.0  36.0     74.5     54.5 15.2
C5     2    1 Vic   f   6    92.5   57.6     91.5  36.5     72.5     51.2 16.0
C10    3    1 Vic   f   6    94.0   60.0     95.5  39.0     75.4     51.9 15.5
C15    4    1 Vic   f   6    93.2   57.1     92.0  38.0     76.1     52.2 15.2
C23    5    1 Vic   f   2    91.5   56.3     85.5  36.0     71.0     53.2 15.1
C24    6    1 Vic   f   1    93.1   54.8     90.5  35.5     73.2     53.6 14.2
    chest belly
C3   28.0    36
C5   28.5    33
C10  30.0    34
C15  28.0    34
C23  28.5    33
C24  30.0    32


The variable Pop (location where possum was trapped) is qualitative. We could use the following ways to display it. We first compute the frequency table of the variable Pop:

table(possum$Pop)


  Vic other 
   46    58 


or relative frequency table:

table(possum$Pop)/length(possum$Pop)


      Vic     other 
0.4423077 0.5576923 


Then we could draw the barplot of this variable:

barplot(table(possum$Pop))








and the pie plot:

pie(table(possum$Pop))








Note that table is handling the statistics, while barplot and pie draw on top of the result from table.




2.4.2 Quantitative (numerical) data
For quantitative data, we would like to see the histogram, as well as computing some statistics: min, max, quartiles, median and mean.

Histogram is an approximation of the distribution. In other words, we split the range into small segments (called bins), and count the frequency or relative frequency of data falling into these bins.
Box plot consists of a box, two lines and possibly some points:

The box in the box plot extends from the lower quartile to the upper quartile. The difference between the upper quartile and the lower quartile is called the inter-quartile range (IQR).
The lines, known as whiskers, extend to one and a half times the interquartile range, but they stop at the most extreme data points that fall within this range.
The points, considered as outliers, are those which are not covered by the box and the lines.


Histograms and boxplots help check normality and outliers before fitting regression. These summaries approximate the underlying distribution of the variable. In regression, we later apply similar tools to the residuals to check model assumptions.

Example 2.4 (possum)  


Click to expand.

We still consider the possum dataset. We could use summary to compute the major statistics.

library(DAAG)
data(possum)
summary(possum)

      case             site          Pop     sex         age       
 Min.   :  1.00   Min.   :1.000   Vic  :46   f:43   Min.   :1.000  
 1st Qu.: 26.75   1st Qu.:1.000   other:58   m:61   1st Qu.:2.250  
 Median : 52.50   Median :3.000                     Median :3.000  
 Mean   : 52.50   Mean   :3.625                     Mean   :3.833  
 3rd Qu.: 78.25   3rd Qu.:6.000                     3rd Qu.:5.000  
 Max.   :104.00   Max.   :7.000                     Max.   :9.000  
                                                    NA's   :2      
    hdlngth           skullw         totlngth         taill      
 Min.   : 82.50   Min.   :50.00   Min.   :75.00   Min.   :32.00  
 1st Qu.: 90.67   1st Qu.:54.98   1st Qu.:84.00   1st Qu.:35.88  
 Median : 92.80   Median :56.35   Median :88.00   Median :37.00  
 Mean   : 92.60   Mean   :56.88   Mean   :87.09   Mean   :37.01  
 3rd Qu.: 94.72   3rd Qu.:58.10   3rd Qu.:90.00   3rd Qu.:38.00  
 Max.   :103.10   Max.   :68.60   Max.   :96.50   Max.   :43.00  
                                                                 
    footlgth        earconch          eye            chest          belly      
 Min.   :60.30   Min.   :40.30   Min.   :12.80   Min.   :22.0   Min.   :25.00  
 1st Qu.:64.60   1st Qu.:44.80   1st Qu.:14.40   1st Qu.:25.5   1st Qu.:31.00  
 Median :68.00   Median :46.80   Median :14.90   Median :27.0   Median :32.50  
 Mean   :68.46   Mean   :48.13   Mean   :15.05   Mean   :27.0   Mean   :32.59  
 3rd Qu.:72.50   3rd Qu.:52.00   3rd Qu.:15.72   3rd Qu.:28.0   3rd Qu.:34.12  
 Max.   :77.90   Max.   :56.20   Max.   :17.80   Max.   :32.0   Max.   :40.00  
 NA's   :1                                                                     


The variable hdlngth (head length) is quantitative. We first show the histogram. We could use breaks to control the number of bins. Note that the function hist does not only draw the histogram; it also provides many useful pieces of information.

res <- hist(possum$hdlngth, breaks=10)







res

$breaks
 [1]  82  84  86  88  90  92  94  96  98 100 102 104

$counts
 [1]  1  6  2 12 22 27 22  7  3  0  2

$density
 [1] 0.004807692 0.028846154 0.009615385 0.057692308 0.105769231 0.129807692
 [7] 0.105769231 0.033653846 0.014423077 0.000000000 0.009615385

$mids
 [1]  83  85  87  89  91  93  95  97  99 101 103

$xname
[1] "possum$hdlngth"

$equidist
[1] TRUE

attr(,"class")
[1] "histogram"


Then we could show the box plot. You may compare the lines in the plot with the summary table and the histogram above.

boxplot(possum$hdlngth)












2.4.3 Relations among multiple variables
We could show the relation between two variables in a scatterplot. Scatterplots help us assess linearity, detect outliers, and decide whether transformation is needed.

Example 2.5 (possum)  


Click to expand.

When both variables are numerical and continuous:

plot(possum$hdlngth, possum$skullw)








When one variable is categorical:

plot(as.factor(possum$Pop), possum$skullw)








Note that in this case, the categorical data has to be a factor. And once it is cast into a factor, the plot is multiple box plots for each category. This type of grouped boxplot corresponds to comparing group means and is directly related to one-way ANOVA and regression with dummy variables. It will be discussed in later sections.
We can see pair plots for each pair of variables. Pair plots are very important because they help reveal relationships among predictors. Note that before creating the plot, we have to cast pop and sex into factors.

possum$Pop <- as.factor(possum$Pop)
possum$sex <- as.factor(possum$sex)
pairs(possum)














[1] Healy, K. (2025). Gssrdoc: General social survey documentation for use in r.


[2] Wickham, H. (2014). Tidy data. Journal of Statistical Software 59.


[3] Mendenhall, W. and Sincich, T. (2020). A second course in statistics: Regression analyisis. Pearson Education, Inc, [Hoboken, New Jersey].

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
19.1 Gemini
This course plan integrates the theoretical depth of The Elements of Statistical Learning (ESL) and the computational focus of Ruoqing Zhu’s SMLR and STAT 432 with the intuitive, application-based approach of An Introduction to Statistical Learning (ISLR).
The curriculum is designed for a 15-week semester, prioritizing numerical optimization (as emphasized in SMLR) alongside standard statistical learning theory.

19.1.1 Phase 1: Foundations & Optimization (Weeks 1–3)
Focus: Setting the stage with R, Linear Algebra, and the “How” of algorithm fitting. * Week 1: Introduction & The Learning Framework * What is Statistical Learning? (ISLR Ch. 2; ESL Ch. 2). * Supervised vs. Unsupervised Learning. * The Bias-Variance Trade-off and MSE (SMLR Ch. 12.3). * Week 2: Computational Toolkit * R & RMarkdown: Environment setup and reproducible workflows (SMLR Ch. 1-2). * Linear Algebra Refresher: Matrix inversion, rank updates, and block matrices (SMLR Ch. 4). * Week 3: Optimization Basics * First and Second-order properties (Gradient vs. Hessian). * Algorithms: Gradient Descent, Newton’s Method, and Coordinate Descent (SMLR Ch. 5). * Lab: Implementing Linear Regression using optim() from scratch.


19.1.2 Phase 2: Linear Models & Regularization (Weeks 4–6)
Focus: Moving beyond Ordinary Least Squares to handle high-dimensional data. * Week 4: Linear Regression & Model Selection * OLS properties and limitations. * Criteria: \(C_p\), AIC, BIC, and Adjusted \(R^2\) (SMLR Ch. 6; ISLR Ch. 6). * Search: Best subset vs. Stepwise methods. * Week 5: Penalized Regression (Ridge & Lasso) * Ridge Regression: Motivation via correlated variables and convexity (SMLR Ch. 7). * Lasso: The \(L_1\) penalty, sparsity, and the soft-thresholding operator (SMLR Ch. 8). * Elastic Net: Combining \(L_1\) and \(L_2\) penalties. * Week 6: Path Algorithms & Cross-Validation * The Lasso solution path and Path-wise Coordinate Descent (SMLR Ch. 8.4). * k-fold and Leave-one-out Cross-Validation (ISLR Ch. 5). * Lab: The glmnet package in R.


19.1.3 Phase 3: Classification & Smoothing (Weeks 7–9)
Focus: Categorical outcomes and non-linear boundaries. * Week 7: Linear Classification * Logistic Regression: Parameters, interpretation, and Newton-Raphson for MLE (SMLR Ch. 10). * Discriminant Analysis (LDA/QDA): Bayes’ Rule and decision boundaries (ISLR Ch. 4; SMLR Ch. 11). * Week 8: Non-parametric Smoothing & Splines * Moving beyond linearity: Polynomials and Piecewise functions. * Splines: Basis functions, Natural Cubic Splines, and Smoothing Splines (SMLR Ch. 9; ISLR Ch. 7). * Week 9: Local Learning & Kernels * K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) for regression and classification (SMLR Ch. 12). * Kernel Density Estimation and Nadaraya-Watson (SMLR Ch. 13). * The “Curse of Dimensionality.”


19.1.4 Phase 4: Support Vector Machines & Kernels (Weeks 10–11)
Focus: The “Kernel Trick” and Maximal Margins. * Week 10: Support Vector Machines (SVM) * The Maximal Margin Classifier and Slack Variables (SMLR Ch. 17). * Duality: From Primal to Dual formulations (ESL Ch. 12). * Week 11: RKHS & Kernel Methods * Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS) basics (SMLR Ch. 15). * Kernel Ridge Regression and the Kernel Trick (SMLR Ch. 16).


19.1.5 Phase 5: Trees, Ensembles & Unsupervised Learning (Weeks 12–15)
Focus: Modern “Black Box” winners and discovery without labels. * Week 12: Tree-Based Methods * Decision Trees: Gini index vs. Entropy (ISLR Ch. 8). * Bagging and Random Forests (SMLR Ch. 19-20). * Week 13: Boosting * AdaBoost and the Stagewise Additive Model. * Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM) and XGBoost/LightGBM concepts (SMLR Ch. 22-23). * Week 14: Unsupervised Learning I (Clustering) * K-means and Hierarchical Clustering (SMLR Ch. 24-25). * Spectral Clustering and the Graph Laplacian (SMLR Ch. 28). * Week 15: Unsupervised Learning II (Dimension Reduction) * Principal Component Analysis (PCA). * Modern visualization: t-SNE and UMAP (SMLR Ch. 31).


19.1.6 Required Reading List

SMLR: Zhu, R. Statistical Machine Learning with R. (Primary for optimization/computation).
ISLR: James, G., et al. An Introduction to Statistical Learning. (Primary for intuition/labs).
ESL: Hastie, T., et al. The Elements of Statistical Learning. (Reference for theoretical derivations).

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
21.1 1. Model Fitting & Inference
While scikit-learn is the industry standard for prediction, statsmodels is the primary library for statistical inference (\(p\)-values, \(R^2\), and \(t\)-tests) and matches the R lm() experience.








Feature
R Code
Python (statsmodels.formula.api)




Linear Regression
fit <- lm(y ~ x1 + x2, data=df)
fit = smf.ols("y ~ x1 + x2", data=df).fit()


Logistic Regression
glm(y ~ x, data=df, family="binomial")
smf.logit("y ~ x", data=df).fit()


Model Summary
summary(fit)
print(fit.summary())


ANOVA Table
anova(fit)
sm.stats.anova_lm(fit)


Predictions
predict(fit, newdata)
fit.predict(new_data)


Interaction Terms
lm(y ~ x1 * x2)
smf.ols("y ~ x1 * x2", data=df).fit()


Polynomials
lm(y ~ poly(x, 2))
smf.ols("y ~ x + I(x**2)", data=df).fit()

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
21.2 2. Model Selection & Regularization
Python’s scikit-learn (sklearn) is the powerhouse for cross-validation and penalized regression.









Feature
R Code (Package)
Python Library
Python Counterpart




Lasso / Ridge
glmnet(x, y)
sklearn
LassoCV() or RidgeCV()


Stepwise (AIC/BIC)
step(fit)
sklearn
SequentialFeatureSelector


Best Subset
regsubsets() (leaps)
mlxtend
ExhaustiveFeatureSelector


Validation Split
sample() / createDataPartition
sklearn
train_test_split()


K-Fold CV
cv.glmnet()
sklearn
cross_val_score() or GridSearchCV()

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
21.3 3. Diagnostics & Residuals
In R, plot(fit) automatically generates four diagnostic plots. In Python, you access these via the .resid attribute or statsmodels.graphics.








Metric / Plot
R Code
Python Code




Residuals
residuals(fit)
fit.resid


Fitted Values
fitted(fit)
fit.fittedvalues


Q-Q Plot
qqnorm(fit$resid)
sm.qqplot(fit.resid, line='s')


Leverage Plot
plot(fit, which=4)
sm.graphics.influence_plot(fit)


Residual Plot
plot(fit, which=1)
sns.residplot(x=fit.fittedvalues, y=df['y'])

title: 1  Course Overview
text:
21.4 4. Required Libraries for the Course
To set up a Python environment equivalent to the R statistical suite, run:
```bash pip install numpy pandas matplotlib seaborn statsmodels scikit-learn mlxtend islp
